Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S promoter (constitutive) for stilbene synthase in transgenic tomato; 35S promoter also mentioned generally for transgene expression contexts
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core entry-point genes of phenylpropanoid metabolism are useful for broad pathway engineering, but manipulation often causes pleiotropy because they feed lignin, flavonoids, coumarins, volatiles, tannins, and defense compounds. Best used with tissue-specific or inducible deployment and with downstream branch engineering rather than indiscriminate constitutive overexpression.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: General phenylpropanoid pathway genes determine carbon flux from shikimate-derived phenylalanine into diverse phenylpropanoid end products.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review synthesis of structural studies, mutants, transgenics, enzyme assays, metabolite profiling, and expression analyses across species
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light variation; nutrient content; biotic and abiotic stressors; photosynthetic status
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Not established in this review for the common entry genes as a general mechanism
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Entry genes show developmental, spatial, organ-specific, and stress-responsive expression; shikimate pathway genes respond to light and nutrient status
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species in native contexts across plants
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: None experimentally consolidated in this review
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative transcript/metabolite analyses cited; no accession numbers reported in the review
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PAL is represented by multigene families: 4 genes in Arabidopsis, 5 in poplar, 9 in rice, ~20 putative genes in tomato. Functional specialization includes lignin, condensed tannin, and flowering-associated roles in poplar. Solanaceae show many putative PAL genes but strong expression of mainly one gene and extensive silencing of others.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong perturbation of entry flux may alter multiple downstream metabolites and growth-related processes.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Phenylalanine/tyrosine ammonia-lyase family; MIO prosthetic group-forming ammonia lyase
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAL catalyzes the nonoxidative deamination of phenylalanine to trans-cinnamate, committing carbon from shikimate metabolism to phenylpropanoids. 2. PAL genes form small-to-large multigene families with organ-, development-, and stress-specific expression. 3. Differential PAL deployment partitions flux into lignin, tannins, and other branches depending on species and tissue.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Species- and organ-specific throughout plant tissues
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural support, UV protection, defense, pigmentation, signaling, nutraceutical properties

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Shikimate-pathway control points can modulate precursor supply for many nutrient and defense metabolites, but are high-risk engineering targets due to central carbon-flow roles.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Shikimate pathway genes regulate supply of aromatic amino acid precursors feeding general phenylpropanoid metabolism.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical localization studies; transcriptional analyses; feedback inhibition studies
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light; nutrient content; phenylalanine feedback status
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Responsive to variations in light or nutrient content; regulation appears tighter and more complex than PAL or CHS
Common_Genes.Gene_Name: DHAP synthase; arogenate/prephenate dehydratase; ICS1; EPSPS
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review treats these as upstream/common precursor genes affecting phenylpropanoid or related aromatic metabolism. ICS1 is highlighted for salicylic acid biosynthesis; EPSPS expression tracks benzenoid volatile formation in petunia.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may affect aromatic amino acid homeostasis, defense, and overall growth.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Primary metabolism enzymes of shikimate/aromatic amino acid pathway
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plastidial for shikimate pathway enzymes generally; plastid proven for arogenate dehydratase/dehydrogenase
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Shikimate pathway precursor supply strongly influences phenylpropanoid output. 2. Regulation occurs through transcriptional and feedback mechanisms. 3. ICS1 provides a species-dependent route to salicylic acid, while EPSPS expression is associated with floral volatile production.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Aromatic precursor pools and salicylic acid/benzenoid-related products
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Plastid-derived precursor supply affects multiple tissues; SA and volatiles accumulate in defense-active tissues and flowers
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Aromatic primary-to-secondary metabolic intermediates; signaling phenolics
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor supply, defense signaling, volatile-mediated attraction/defense

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: C4H is essential and unsuitable for blunt overexpression/knockout in breeding because single-gene loss causes severe developmental defects. Fine-tuning may redirect unusual soluble metabolites but carries major growth penalties.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates trans-cinnamate at the 4-position to form 4-coumarate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for normal phenylpropanoid flux; mutation causes severe growth and developmental defects and accumulation of cinnamoylmalate in leaves.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Single-gene mutant analysis and metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms central intermediate required for nearly all downstream phenylpropanoid end products
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specified beyond general pathway stress/light regulation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Native Arabidopsis expression implied in phenylpropanoid tissues
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolome changes in mutants discussed; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Among ~300 functional Arabidopsis P450 genes, cinnamate 4-hydroxylation is encoded by a single gene, underscoring low redundancy at this step.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Central core pathway before branching to lignin, flavonoid, coumarin, volatiles, etc.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe growth and development defects when impaired.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key obligatory entry step; effectively essential control point
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP73A5 is the sole Arabidopsis C4H gene mediating 4-hydroxylation of cinnamate. 2. Disruption severely impairs growth and development. 3. Loss of function redirects flux to unusual cinnamoylmalate accumulation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: 4CL genes are branch-routing tools for engineering lignin, flavonoids, and related pathways. Use isoform-specific targeting to avoid broad metabolic disruption.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Activates hydroxycinnamic acids to corresponding CoA thioesters, especially 4-coumaroyl CoA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Channels aromatic CoA esters into distinct phenylpropanoid branches; establishes 4-coumaroyl CoA as major branchpoint intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization and comparative gene family analyses summarized in the review
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool and branchpoint intermediate for flavonoids, lignin, and other phenylpropanoid products
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Encoded by small gene families across species
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Substrate specificity of many 4CL ligases influences whether certain CoA esters, e.g., sinapoyl-CoA, contribute to lignin in a species-dependent way.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering 4CL can rewire multiple downstream branches with broad developmental consequences.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 4CL activates hydroxycinnamates to CoA esters. 2. 4-Coumaroyl-CoA is a central branchpoint for major phenylpropanoid products. 3. Isoform specificity helps determine lignin versus flavonoid and other branch fluxes.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; tomato; Pinus radiata cell cultures
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum lycopersicum; Pinus radiata
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: HCT/SHT family members are strong engineering targets for lignin composition and chlorogenic acid enhancement. Tissue-specific manipulation is recommended because stem effects can be strong while leaves are less affected. Useful for biofuel/feed quality and antioxidant enrichment.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers hydroxycinnamoyl groups from CoA esters to shikimate/quinate, generating hydroxycinnamoyl shikimate/quinate esters.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Down-regulation increases H lignin units, depletes S units, and elevates caffeoylquinate esters; in tomato, HCT expression increases chlorogenic acid; in pine cultures SHT silencing strongly alters lignin quantity and quality toward H-type monolignols.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene down-regulation/silencing; transgenic expression; metabolite and lignin composition profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate intermediates for meta-hydroxylation and controls branching between lignin and soluble caffeoyl esters
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Photosynthetic status and shikimate/quinate availability are proposed to influence pathway flux interpretation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Stem-associated strong impact; minor leaf effects in some down-regulation studies
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Native expression in lignifying tissues and species-specific organs
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolite profiling used; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Belongs to BAHD acyltransferases and may be regulated at multiple levels. High in vitro Km for shikimate/quinate suggests flux sensitivity to precursor availability.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Hydroxycinnamoyl shikimate/quinate module of monolignol and chlorogenic acid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can shift lignin structure substantially, affecting mechanical properties; broad effects on soluble phenylpropanoid pools.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Hydroxycinnamoyl shikimate/quinate esters; caffeoylquinate-related metabolites downstream
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA esters plus shikimate or quinate
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum lycopersicum; Pinus radiata
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HCT/SHT creates shikimate/quinate esters required for downstream aromatic ring meta-hydroxylation. 2. Reduced HCT/SHT activity redirects lignin composition toward H units and raises caffeoylquinate esters. 3. Overexpression can be exploited to increase chlorogenic acid in tomato or alter lignin quality in woody systems.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Stems/vascular tissues for lignin; leaves/fruits/tubers depending on chlorogenic acid engineering context

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CYP98 enzymes are powerful branch-shaping targets but require careful tissue-specific use because they influence lignin, soluble esters, and tapetum-specific metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: CYP98A3 hydroxylates hydroxycinnamoyl shikimate/quinate esters at the meta-position; CYP98A8/A9 modify acylspermidine-related intermediates in tapetum-specific metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CYP98A3 is essential for caffeoyl ester formation in lignin-related metabolism; CYP98A8 knockout shifts acylated spermidine conjugates from 5-hydroxyferuloyl to feruloyl forms; CYP98A8/A9 underlie evolution of a new tapetum-specific phenylpropanoid branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Substrate assays; knockout mutant metabolite profiling; evolutionary and expression analyses
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor patterns required for methylated lignin precursors and specialized acylspermidine products
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Photosynthetic status proposed to affect flux through shikimate/quinate ester route
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: p-Coumaroyl shikimate/quinate 3'-hydroxylase and related CYP98 monooxygenases
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: CYP98A8/A9 are tapetum/flower-bud associated; CYP98A3 acts in established lignin-related pathway
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS/UPLC-MS metabolite profiling; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP98A8/A9 arose from duplication of a CYP98A3-like ancestor and evolved under positive selection, illustrating rapid pathway innovation.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Lignin meta-hydroxylation module; tapetum acylspermidine branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbation may affect lignin composition and pollen/anther metabolite composition.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Caffeoyl shikimate/quinate-type intermediates; hydroxylated acylspermidine precursors
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: 4-Coumaroyl shikimate/quinate esters and related acylspermidine intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP98A3 specifically hydroxylates shikimate/quinate esters, not free acids or CoA/glucose esters. 2. CYP98A8/A9 were recruited to a tapetum-specific acylspermidine pathway. 3. Loss of CYP98A8 alters the hydroxylation state of major spermidine conjugates.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Lignin-related caffeoyl derivatives; acylated spermidine conjugates
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cell wall biosynthesis; reproductive tissue specialization

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CCoAOMT1 is a hub enzyme useful for redirecting methoxylated phenylpropanoid profiles, but hub status means high pleiotropic risk. Engineering substrate promiscuity of OMTs may help tailor lignin composition.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methylates caffeoyl-CoA and related catechol-containing phenylpropanoid intermediates; AtTSM1 methylates 5-hydroxyferuloyl moieties in acylspermidine conjugates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CCoAOMT1 contributes to monolignol formation and also sinapoylmalate, scopoletin, and acylspermidine biosynthesis; knockout strongly reduces acylspermidine formation. AtTSM1 catalyzes the final methylation step in tapetal acylspermidine production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Knockout analysis; enzyme assays; metabolite profiling; structural characterization for related OMTs
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies methylated intermediates required for lignin, coumarin, sinapate ester, and acylpolyamine end products
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Root-specific scopoletin context; flower-bud/tapetum-specific acylspermidine context
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: CCoAOMT1 highly expressed in all organs from seeds to roots to flowers; AtTSM1 exclusively in tapetum of young flower buds
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS/UPLC-MS metabolite profiling; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CCoAOMT1 has shifted from a lignin-only annotation to a multi-pathway modifier. AtTSM1 was recruited as a distinct but related enzyme for specialized tapetal metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Monolignol methylation; coumarin branch; acylspermidine branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering hub methyltransferases can broadly perturb soluble and structural phenolics.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Central modification hub, not strictly rate-limiting but key control point
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CCoAOMT1 methylates central phenylpropanoid intermediates beyond lignin biosynthesis. 2. It is required for normal scopoletin and acylspermidine accumulation. 3. AtTSM1 executes a specialized terminal methylation step in tapetum-specific spermidine conjugate formation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Lignin monomers; sinapoylmalate; scopoletin; acylated spermidines
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cell wall formation, UV protection, reproduction-associated metabolites

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: F5H is an excellent target to alter syringyl/guaiacyl ratio and lignin properties for biomass processing. Must be balanced because knockdown induces wider metabolome perturbation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates ferulate/coniferylaldehyde-related lignin precursors at the 5-position to promote syringyl monolignol formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression shifts lignin strongly toward syringyl-rich lignin in poplar; knockdown causes pleiotropic Arabidopsis metabolome changes.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression and knockdown coupled with lignin/metabolome profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Diverts flux toward syringyl lignin branch
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolome analyses referenced; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Critical determinant of angiosperm lignin structure and physical properties.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 5-Hydroxylated lignin precursors leading to syringyl monomers
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F5H strongly controls syringyl lignin formation. 2. Overexpression can make poplar lignin nearly exclusively syringyl-type. 3. Reduced F5H perturbs the wider phenylpropanoid metabolome.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CCR1 and CAD families are major lignin engineering targets for biomass quality. Partial reduction can lower lignin and redirect flux to soluble phenolics, but may affect structural integrity.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: CCR reduces cinnamoyl-CoA esters to cinnamaldehydes; CAD reduces cinnamaldehydes to corresponding monolignol alcohols.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CCR1 knockout reduces lignin and shifts soluble phenolics from sinapoyl malate toward feruloyl malate; individual CAD gene insertions usually have only marginal effects on total lignin composition in Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Knockout and insertion-line analyses with lignin and soluble metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Committed steps directly forming monolignol precursors and monolignol alcohols for lignin/lignan biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: CAD family shows temporal and organ-specific complexity
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CCR1; CCR2; CAD family genes not individually specified
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Functional genomics and genetic mapping discussed; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CAD is traditionally a key step, but many Arabidopsis CAD genes appear functionally redundant or may not all be bona fide CADs.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced lignin can impair structural properties; broad shifts in soluble ester pools occur.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Committed steps; CAD not rate-limiting in Arabidopsis single-gene context
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CCR and CAD execute the two reduction steps into monolignol biosynthesis. 2. CCR1 loss lowers lignin and redirects flux to feruloyl malate. 3. Individual CAD gene disruption often causes only minor lignin effects, implying redundancy or misannotation among family members.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SCPL acyltransferases such as SCT/SMT are good targets to reduce antinutritional sinapine in seeds or redirect sinapate esters to seedling-protective compounds. Editing active-site residues may alter glucose-ester utilization.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers sinapoyl groups from 1-O-sinapoylglucose to choline or malate to form sinapine or sinapoylmalate, respectively.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SCT supports sinapine accumulation in seeds; SMT supports sinapoylmalate accumulation in seedlings.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Molecular characterization, structural analysis, and enzyme activity assays
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: SCT in seeds; SMT in seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SMT evolved from serine carboxypeptidase-like proteins and retains catalytic triad; Asp/Glu substitution near catalytic Ser enables glucose-ester substrate binding.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing sinapine could affect seed-associated phenolic storage and seedling UV-protective sinapate ester dynamics.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SCT and SMT use sinapoylglucose rather than CoA esters as acyl donors. 2. They determine whether sinapate is stored as sinapine in seeds or sinapoylmalate in seedlings. 3. Small active-site changes underlie their divergence from hydrolytic SCP ancestors.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Storage/antinutritional seed metabolite; UV-protective soluble phenylpropanoid

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: SHT and associated CYP/OMT genes can be used to build tissue-specific specialized metabolite pathways, especially in reproductive tissues, but agronomic value remains unclear because fertility and whole-plant phenotype were not obviously affected.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers hydroxycinnamic acids to spermidine to form hydroxycinnamoyl spermidine conjugates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SHT knockout eliminates trisacyl spermidine conjugates from anthers and pollen grains.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Knockout mutant analysis with LC-MS/UPLC-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms core polyamine-conjugated terminal products
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The sequence of initial hydroxylation/methylation steps before/with SHT action is not fully resolved.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious fertility or whole-plant phenotype reported despite loss of these conjugates.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Hydroxycinnamic acids (as activated donors in pathway context) and spermidine
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Essential pathway step for product accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SHT catalyzes the core transfer step producing acylated spermidine conjugates. 2. Knockout plants lack trisacyl conjugates in anthers and pollen. 3. The pathway exemplifies recruitment of duplicated enzymes into organ-specific specialized metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Anthers, pollen grains, tapetum of flower buds
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Putative UV protection, fertility support, defense

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Co-expression in transgenic Torenia hybrida; promoter details not specified
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AmSA1 plus chalcone 4'-O-glucosyltransferase is a practical engineering module for yellow flower color creation in ornamentals, especially when combined with anthocyanin down-regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Aureosidin synthase oxidatively converts 4'-O-glucosylated chalcones to aurone 6-O-glucosides in the vacuole; chalcone 4'-O-glucosyltransferase glycosylates chalcones to enable vacuolar transport and conversion.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Co-expression yields bright yellow aurone-containing petals; both enzymes are required for effective aurone accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic co-expression and flower color/metabolite analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: UGT co-expression was necessary to transport 4'-O-glycosylated chalcones into vacuoles where AmSA1 acts.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requires simultaneous suppression of competing anthocyanin branch for visible yellow outcome.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Aurone biosynthesis in petals requires both chalcone 4'-O-glucosylation and vacuolar aureosidin synthase activity. 2. Co-expression in Torenia generated bright yellow flowers. 3. Transport competence of the glycosylated precursor is essential for product formation.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Gerbera hybrida; Rosa sp.; Vitis vinifera
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ANS, DFR, hydroxylases, glycosyltransferases, and OMTs are central pigment engineering targets for flower and fruit color modification. Gene stacking is usually required for desired hue, stability, and vacuolar chemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: ANS oxidizes leucoanthocyanidins in anthocyanidin formation but may also show flavonol synthase-related activity; DFR reduces dihydroflavonols; 3',5'-hydroxylase adds B-ring hydroxyls; bifunctional rose UGT glycosylates 3- and 5-OH positions; grapevine OMT methylates vicinal dihydroxy anthocyanins from petunidin to malvidin type.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: These enzymes determine anthocyanin composition, blue/purple coloration potential, and pigment stabilization. Transgenic expression of Viola 3',5'-hydroxylase plus Iris DFR in rose caused near-exclusive delphinidin accumulation and bluish petals.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression, enzyme assays, metabolite/color profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target pigments or decorates them for hue/stability
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Petal developmental context; vacuolar pH/metal complexation/co-pigmentation affect final color
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Anthocyanidin synthase; dihydroflavonol reductase; flavonoid 3',5'-hydroxylase; anthocyanin glucosyltransferase; anthocyanin O-methyltransferase
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Histone modification linkage reported for maize R-regulated anthocyanin system, not for these enzymes directly
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Name: ANS; DFR; F3'5'H; rose bifunctional UGT; grapevine anthocyanin OMT
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Functionally connected with anthocyanin regulatory TF complexes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis ANS catalytic assignment has been questioned because of apparent flavonol synthase activity; co-expression of an acyltransferase alone did not further intensify blue coloration in engineered roses.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Anthocyanidin formation and late anthocyanin decoration
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Color engineering can require multi-gene balancing; changes may affect co-pigment pools and other flavonoid branches.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Anthocyanidins, delphinidin, glycosylated and methylated anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Leucoanthocyanidins; dihydroflavonols; anthocyanins with vicinal dihydroxy groups
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: 2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase; SDR reductase; cytochrome P450; UGT; OMT
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis; Viola spp.; Iris hollandica; Rosa sp.; Vitis vinifera
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Viola spp.; Iris × hollandica; Rosa sp.; Vitis vinifera
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Late anthocyanin enzymes determine pigment hue, hydroxylation, glycosylation, and methylation. 2. Stacked transgenes for hydroxylation and reduction can create bluish rose petals with delphinidin accumulation. 3. Some enzyme assignments, such as ANS specificity, remain more flexible than once assumed.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Petals and pigmented tissues, mainly vacuoles

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Bellis perennis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Specialized glycosyltransferases such as UGT89C1, UGT94B1, and OsCGT1 enable targeted decoration of flavonoids to improve stability, solubility, and bioactivity in crops.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: UGT89C1 transfers rhamnose to the 7-O position of flavonols; UGT94B1 is a glucuronosyltransferase; OsCGT1 catalyzes flavone C-glucosylation likely via a 2-hydroxyflavone/open-chain intermediate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: These enzymes define specific flavonoid glycosides and thereby diversify stable end products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Correlation of transcript and metabolite profiles, reverse genetics, biochemical characterization
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: UGT89C1 linked to late flavonol biosynthesis; others species/tissue specific
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcript-metabolite correlation studies; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: OsCGT1 lacked an obvious CGT-specific motif by sequence and could not be found by simple homology searching.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Glycosylation changes can alter bioavailability and compartmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Bellis perennis; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Transcript-metabolite integration enabled identification of UGT89C1 as Arabidopsis flavonol 7-O-rhamnosyltransferase. 2. UGT94B1 established rare plant glucuronosylation. 3. OsCGT1 revealed the molecular basis of flavone C-glycosylation in rice.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Humulus lupulus; Medicago sativa / M. truncatula / Lotus japonicus; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: These branch enzymes are strong targets for nutraceutical engineering: CGT for C-glycosides, prenyltransferase for prenylated flavonoids, isoflavone synthase/OMTs for phytoalexins and health-related isoflavonoids, and stilbene synthase for resveratrol biofortification in edible crops.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Naringenin 8-prenyltransferase adds prenyl group to naringenin; isoflavone synthase catalyzes aryl migration from liquiritigenin to isoflavonoid scaffold; 4'-OMT methylates isoflavonoid intermediates and pterocarpans; stilbene synthase condenses p-coumaroyl-CoA/malonyl-CoA to resveratrol-type stilbenes; hop 6'-O-desmethylxanthohumol OMT methylates prenylchalcone intermediate for xanthohumol stabilization.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Control accumulation of prenylated chalcones, isoflavonoids, phytoalexins, and stilbenes such as resveratrol/piceid. 35S-driven stilbene synthase in tomato fruits yields substantial stilbene accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EST-guided cloning, biochemical assays, transgenic expression, metabolite profiling, structural characterization
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms specialized end products or decisive branch intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glandular trichomes in hop; petals/fruits in engineering contexts; phytoalexin-inducing stress context in legumes implied
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Naringenin 8-prenyltransferase; isoflavone synthase; isoflavonoid O-methyltransferase; stilbene synthase; 6'-O-desmethylxanthohumol O-methyltransferase
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Hop glandular trichomes for xanthohumol branch; tomato fruits for transgenic stilbene accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: EST approach in hop; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A single promiscuous 4'-isoflavonoid OMT can methylate both an early and a late pathway intermediate due to similar 3D structures. STS engineering is attractive because a single transgene can install stilbene production.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Novel pathway installation may perturb precursor pools; health benefits in vivo remain uncertain despite strong in vitro evidence.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Naringenin; liquiritigenin; isoflavonoid intermediates; p-coumaroyl-CoA and malonyl-CoA; desmethylxanthohumol intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Membrane prenyltransferase; cytochrome P450; OMT; type III polyketide synthase
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Rice; hop; legumes; grapevine-derived STS transgene contexts not specified in review
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Humulus lupulus; Medicago spp./Lotus japonicus; source species for STS not specified
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Membrane-associated prenyltransferase; ER for P450s; cytosol for OMT/STS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Specialized branch enzymes install prenylated flavonoids, isoflavonoids, and stilbenes with high nutraceutical or defense value. 2. Hop trichome OMT stabilizes xanthohumol after prenylation. 3. A single 35S-driven stilbene synthase is sufficient to accumulate substantial stilbene levels in tomato fruit.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense, phytoalexin activity, phytoestrogenic and putative health-promoting properties

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ANR/LAR and vacuolar transporters can be used to enhance proanthocyanidin content in crops for nutritional or anti-herbivory traits, but polymerization/deposition mechanisms and bioavailability tradeoffs must be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Anthocyanidin reductase forms epicatechin-type flavan-3-ols; leucoanthocyanidin reductase forms catechin-type flavan-3-ols; TT12/MATE transporters move precursors across vacuolar membranes; TT10 laccase is implicated in oxidative polymerization.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Initiates proanthocyanidin branch and enables seed-coat accumulation/deposition of oligomeric tannins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic analyses of TT loci, biochemical pathway studies, transporter characterization
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms starter monomers, supports transport, and may catalyze polymerization/storage
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Anthocyanidin reductase; leucoanthocyanidin reductase; MATE transporter; laccase-like oxidase
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Seed coat and vacuolar deposition tissues
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Regulated by TT2; TT8; TTG1 complex
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Whether polymerization is enzymatic (TT10/laccase) or non-enzymatic remains unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced tannin may reduce palatability or nutrient bioavailability.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Epicatechin and catechin derivatives; transported vacuolar precursors
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Anthocyanidins; leucoanthocyanidins; PA precursors for transport
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANR and LAR initiate proanthocyanidin biosynthesis from different flavanol stereochemical routes. 2. TT12-mediated transport is required for vacuolar deposition. 3. TT10 may participate in polymerization, though non-enzymatic assembly remains possible.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense, antioxidant potential, seed coat traits

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Petunia hybrida; Antirrhinum majus; Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: BA/SA pathway enzymes are key targets for disease-resistance engineering and floral scent modification, but species-specific pathway architecture requires comparative validation before deployment.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: A 3-keto-acyl-CoA thiolase participates in β-oxidative BA synthesis in petunia; benzaldehyde dehydrogenase forms BA in a non-β-oxidative route in snapdragon; benzoic acid 2-hydroxylase converts BA to SA in tobacco; ICS1 converts chorismate to isochorismate in an alternative SA route; SABATH methyltransferases produce methyl benzoate and methyl salicylate; SABP2 methylesterase hydrolyzes methyl salicylate during SAR signaling; SA UGTs glycosylate SA mainly at the carboxyl group.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: These enzymes determine benzenoid volatile emission and salicylic-acid-based defense signaling; modification of SA affects pathogen susceptibility.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Feeding studies, mutant analysis (sid2/ICS1), RNA silencing, enzyme identification, volatile profiling
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms BA/SA or their active transport/signaling derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Diurnal and hormonal control; pathogen infection; SAR induction
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: 3-keto-acyl-CoA thiolase; benzaldehyde dehydrogenase; benzoic acid 2-hydroxylase; isochorismate synthase 1; SABATH methyltransferases; SABP2 methylesterase; SA glycosyltransferases
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Petunia floral volatile pathway under diurnal/hormonal control
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: ICS1 (SID2); NahG mentioned as prokaryotic BA2H reference
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Name: ICS1; SABP2; SABATH MTs; BA2H; benzaldehyde dehydrogenase
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evidence supports coexistence of β-oxidative and non-β-oxidative BA pathways, with relative flux varying by species and condition. Arabidopsis relies strongly on ICS1 for SA, whereas tobacco biochemical evidence supports a BA-derived route.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: C6-C1 benzenoid and SA signaling branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulating SA metabolism can increase pathogen susceptibility or alter volatile traits.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Benzoic acid; salicylic acid; methyl salicylate; methyl benzoate; SA glucosides
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: trans-Cinnamate; benzaldehyde; benzoic acid; chorismate; salicylic acid
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Thiolase; aldehyde dehydrogenase; P450; chorismate pathway enzyme; SABATH MT; esterase; UGT
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Species-dependent key control steps, especially ICS1 for Arabidopsis SA
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Petunia hybrida; Antirrhinum majus; Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Varied; ICS1 plastidial implied; SABP2 soluble/cellular
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Multiple BA biosynthetic routes can coexist in one plant. 2. SA biosynthesis is species-dependent, with ICS1 central in Arabidopsis and BA-derived routes evidenced in tobacco. 3. Methylation and demethylation of SA are essential for SAR signaling and volatile communication.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Benzoic acid; salicylic acid; methyl salicylate; methyl benzoate
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Flowers for volatiles; infected/defense tissues for SA
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, systemic acquired resistance, floral scent

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Petunia hybrida; Ocimum basilicum; Pimpinella anisum
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CAAT, EGS, and phenylpropene OMTs are attractive for scent engineering and biocontrol-related volatile enhancement. Combining these with TF engineering can co-improve floral color and scent.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Coniferyl alcohol acetyltransferase acetylates coniferyl alcohol to coniferyl acetate; eugenol synthase reduces coniferyl acetate to eugenol/isoeugenol-type phenylpropenes; specific OMTs methylate 4-hydroxy groups of phenylpropenes; a CCoAOMT1-like protein methylates the 3-OH earlier at caffeoyl-CoA level.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: These enzymes control production of antimicrobial and aromatic phenylpropenes such as eugenol, isoeugenol, and chavicol in floral or glandular tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical pathway elucidation, structural biology of EGS, systems biology integration of transcript/protein/metabolite data
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms volatile end products and their decorated derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Strict diurnal and hormonal control of floral benzenoid/phenylpropene emission
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Coniferyl alcohol acetyltransferase; eugenol synthase; chavicol/phenylpropene O-methyltransferases
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Corolla of petunia; peltate glands of basil; leaves in anise; glandular trichomes in basil for some OMTs
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Functionally linked to upstream CCoAOMT1-like methyltransferase
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome, proteome, and volatile metabolite profiling in basil; no accession numbers
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PAL and CVOMT are additionally controlled post-translationally by phosphorylation or ubiquitinylation, respectively.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Diverting precursors to volatiles may alter lignin/flavonoid pools; scent changes may affect pollinator interactions.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Eugenol; isoeugenol; chavicol and methylated derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Coniferyl alcohol; coniferyl acetate; phenylpropene intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Petunia hybrida; Ocimum basilicum; Pimpinella anisum
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Phenylpropenes are formed from coniferyl alcohol via acetylation and NADPH-dependent reduction. 2. EGS structure clarified the catalytic basis of this reductive step. 3. Organ-specific OMTs and upstream controls shape species-specific volatile bouquets.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: F6'H1 is a precise target to boost scopoletin/scopolin in roots for defense or medicinal metabolite enhancement, especially when paired with upstream feruloyl-CoA supply engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates feruloyl-CoA at the 6' position to form a 6'-hydroxylated trans intermediate that isomerizes and lactonizes to scopoletin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: F6'H1 is the decisive step for scopoletin biosynthesis in Arabidopsis roots; CCoAOMT1 knockout reducing scopoletin supports feruloyl-CoA as precursor.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms immediate precursor of scopoletin
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This key ortho-hydroxylation is catalyzed by a 2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase, not a CYP monooxygenase.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter root phenolic balance and downstream glycosides.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F6'H1 performs the decisive ortho-hydroxylation step of scopoletin biosynthesis. 2. The reaction proceeds from feruloyl-CoA rather than free acids. 3. Coumarin substitution diversity in other species may arise from orthologs with altered substrate specificity.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S promoter for transgenic tomato STS context mentioned generally; not specific to these PKSs
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Rice; Curcuma longa; Hypericum perforatum; Maloideae; Musaceae
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Curcuma longa; Hypericum perforatum; Malus-related Maloideae taxa; Musaceae spp.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Type III PKSs such as curcumin synthase-like enzymes, BPS, and BIS are promising tools for engineering high-value medicinal phenolics in heterologous crops or cell systems.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Rice/Curcuma type III PKS forms bisdemethoxycurcumin from p-coumaroyl-CoA and malonyl-CoA; benzophenone synthase and biphenyl synthase use benzoyl-CoA starter units to form benzophenone/biphenyl scaffolds; a distinct type III PKS is proposed for diarylheptanoids/phenylphenalenones.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: These PKSs initiate the skeleton formation of curcumins, benzophenones, xanthones, biphenyls, dibenzofurans, and related exotic metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme characterization and species-specific pathway studies
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms core scaffold of terminal specialized metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Phytoalexin context for some benzophenone/biphenyl products
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Curcumin synthase-like type III polyketide synthase; benzophenone synthase; biphenyl synthase
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The oxygenation order in curcumin biosynthesis remains unresolved. The general phenylpropanoid pathway upstream is conserved across established and exotic species.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Heterologous production will require precursor balancing and may affect endogenous polyketide metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Rice; Curcuma longa; Hypericum spp.; Maloideae taxa; Musaceae
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Curcuma longa; Hypericum perforatum; Maloideae spp.; Musaceae spp.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Specialized type III PKSs build the carbon skeletons of several exotic phenylpropanoid-derived metabolites. 2. Curcumin-forming PKS activity originally described in rice is also present in Curcuma longa. 3. BPS and BIS use benzoyl-CoA to generate defense-related benzophenone and biphenyl scaffolds.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Overexpression in potato tubers; promoter not specified in excerpt
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: StMtf1 is a useful transcription-factor target for increasing chlorogenic acid in potato tubers; best deployed in storage organs to enhance antioxidant value while minimizing whole-plant pleiotropy.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increases chlorogenic acid accumulation in potato tubers.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts as a positive regulator capable of elevating a major nutraceutical hydroxycinnamate ester in tubers.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Phenylpropanoid ester biosynthesis / chlorogenic acid accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used as an alternative to direct HCT expression for chlorogenic acid enhancement.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential broader transcriptional effects not detailed in this review.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Not specified; likely chlorogenic acid pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes expression of genes favoring chlorogenic acid accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of StMtf1 raises chlorogenic acid in potato tubers. 2. TF-based regulation can be used instead of direct enzyme overexpression to reprogram phenylpropanoid ester accumulation. 3. This highlights transcriptional leverage over nutraceutical hydroxycinnamate biosynthesis.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Transgenic expression in Petunia flowers; promoter not specified
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: PAP1 is a high-value engineering regulator for simultaneous enhancement of flower color and scent; useful for ornamental breeding and possibly volatile-based crop priming strategies, but off-target pathway activation should be expected.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression of PAP1 in petunia flowers increases anthocyanin formation and dramatically elevates phenylpropanoid volatile emission.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression with color and volatile metabolite analysis
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: A strong positive regulator that unexpectedly co-activates both anthocyanin pigmentation and scent-related phenylpropene/phenylpropanoid volatile pathways.
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Not directly for PAP1; anthocyanin regulatory system is linked to chromatin factors in other contexts
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: bHLH factors; TTG/basic complexes (general anthocyanin regulatory network)
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin biosynthesis and phenylpropanoid volatile production
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Volatile metabolite analysis; no accession numbers
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Demonstrates that a single TF can coordinate color and scent traits, broadening ornamental metabolic engineering possibilities.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad activation of phenylpropanoid metabolism may reallocate carbon and alter multiple downstream compounds.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin structural genes; volatile pathway genes not individually specified
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions within MYB-bHLH-WD40-type anthocyanin regulatory context
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates anthocyanin pathway genes and stimulates genes supporting phenylpropanoid volatile formation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAP1 is a potent positive regulator of anthocyanin biosynthesis. 2. In petunia, PAP1 also strongly enhances phenylpropanoid volatile production. 3. A single TF can therefore co-engineer color and scent.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Zea mays; various species
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MYB-bHLH-WD40 regulators are master switches for anthocyanin/proanthocyanidin traits and are prime tools for biofortification and ornamental breeding. Repressors and chromatin context must be managed to avoid unstable or ectopic expression.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: These regulators positively or negatively control anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin accumulation, affecting pigmentation and tannin deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic mutants, overexpression, interaction studies, and metabolite analyses summarized in the review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: They constitute the central regulatory modules and switches governing flavonoid pigment and tannin branch activation or repression.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hormonal control mentioned; developmental seed coat and flower contexts
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: The bHLH domain of maize R interacts with an EMSY-like factor linked to chromatin repair/histone modification, connecting flavonoid regulation to chromatin state.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Anthocyanin tissues, seed coat, flowers, and developmentally regulated organs
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Single-repeat R3 MYBs likely repress by competitive interference with activator complex assembly
Regulation_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: TT2; TT8; TTG1; PAP1/2; maize R
Regulation_Genes.Gene_Name: TT2; TT8; TTG1; PAP1/2; R3 MYB repressors; maize R
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: TT2; TT8; TTG1; bHLH proteins; EMSY-like factor
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Co-expression and metabolomics approaches mentioned; no accession numbers
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: R3 MYB repressors compete with R2R3-MYBs for bHLH binding and counteract PAP1/2-like enhancers. Overexpression of TT2/TT8/TTG1-type activators may create crops with elevated proanthocyanidins.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong activation may alter carbon partitioning, color, tannin content, palatability, and developmental programs.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin/proanthocyanidin structural genes including late flavonoid pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Yes; MYB-bHLH-WD40 complexes are experimentally supported; maize R interacts with EMSY-like chromatin factor
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: R2R3-MYB; R3-MYB; bHLH; WD40 repeat proteins
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation or repression via complex formation/competition
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activators induce late flavonoid genes; R3-MYB repressors dampen these outputs by competing for bHLH binding
Regulation_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Zea mays; various species
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB-bHLH-WD40 complexes are the core regulatory module for anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin biosynthesis. 2. R3-MYB proteins repress pigmentation by interfering with activator complex formation. 3. Chromatin-associated factors can interface with these regulators, linking metabolite accumulation to epigenetic control.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: R2R3-MYB and WRKY candidates may be leveraged to modify suberin/cutin composition for seed coat, drought, and barrier traits, but functional validation remains incomplete.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate regulators are associated with composition, transport, and assembly of suberin/cutin-related mixed polymers containing phenylpropanoid-derived aromatics.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genomic association/candidate identification; no direct functional proof detailed
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Conditionally important candidate regulators, but not yet proven master switches.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Suberin-producing tissues such as cork cambium/endodermis are implicated
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Genomic approach toward suberin biosynthesis/cork differentiation; no accession numbers
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes these are candidates awaiting structural and functional characterization.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulating barrier polymers may alter water relations, pathogen defense, and seed traits.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Not specified; likely cytochrome P450 and BAHD HC-transferase families and transport/assembly genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Presumed activation/repression of suberin biosynthetic and transport genes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Candidate R2R3-MYB and WRKY regulators have been linked to suberin/cutin-related pathways. 2. They may coordinate aromatic and aliphatic polymer assembly genes. 3. Functional evidence is still limited, making them provisional regulatory genes.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Endodermis, cork cambium, seed coat-associated barriers
